title: Systems of Linear Equations
blurb: Two lines at once — solving by graphing, substitution, and elimination, and what the intersection means.

lessons.title: Two Equations, One Answer
lessons.concreteComparison: A riddle: “I'm thinking of two numbers. They add to $7$, and their difference is $3$.” One clue alone has endless answers — $(1,6), (2,5), (0,7)\ldots$ — but BOTH clues together pin down exactly one pair: $5$ and $2$. That's a **system of equations**: two conditions that must hold at once, and the solution is whatever satisfies both.
lessons.objective: Understand a system of two linear equations, and check whether a candidate pair (x, y) satisfies BOTH equations.
concept: A **system of equations** is two (or more) equations about the SAME variables, both required at once: $\begin{cases}x+y=7\\ x-y=3\end{cases}$
A **solution of the system** is a pair $(x,y)$ that makes **both** equations true. $(5,2)$: $5+2=7$ ✓ and $5-2=3$ ✓ — solution. $(6,1)$: first ✓ but $6-1=5\neq3$ ✗ — not a solution.
One linear equation has infinitely many solutions (a whole line of them). The second equation cuts that freedom down — usually to a single point.
Graphically: each equation is a line, and the system's solution is where the lines **intersect** — the one point lying on both.
lessons.keyIdea: A system's solution must satisfy BOTH equations — graphically, the point where the two lines cross.

workedExamples.statement: Is $(5,2)$ a solution of the system $x+y=7$, $x-y=3$?
Is $(6,1)$ a solution of the same system?
workedExamples.solution: $6+1=7$ ✓ but $6-1=5\neq3$ ✗ — no. Passing one equation isn't enough.

commonMistakes.correction: A system's solution must pass BOTH tests — $(6,1)$ fits $x+y=7$ but fails $x-y=3$, so it's out.

commonMistakes.text: Swapping x and y in the pair.
commonMistakes.correction: $(5,2)$ means $x=5$, $y=2$ — order matters; $(2,5)$ is a different claim.

steps.title: Two clues at once
beats: “Two numbers add to $7$; their difference is $3$.”

steps.title: Two lines, one shared point
steps.teach: Each equation draws a line. The **intersection** is the only point on both — the system's solution. Adjust the blue line and watch the solution move with it.

steps.title: Test the pair
steps.prompt: Is $(3,5)$ a solution of the system $y=x+2$ and $y=2x-1$?

steps.title: Check BOTH equations
beats: Both pass → solution. One failure → rejected.

steps.title: Solution or not?
steps.intro: Substitute the pair into each equation.

problems.explanation: $7+3=10$ ✓ and $7-3=4$ ✓; the others fail the difference.

problems.prompt: A system's solution, on a graph, is…
problems.explanation: The intersection is the one point on BOTH lines.

steps.title: Systems built the GPS in your pocket
steps.body: Your phone finds you by solving a system: each satellite says “you're this far from me” — one equation per satellite. One satellite narrows you to a sphere, two to a circle, three to points, four to YOU. Every blue dot on a map is a solved system.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A system = two equations that must hold **at once**.
On a graph: the intersection point of the two lines.

lessons.title: Solving by Graphing
lessons.concreteComparison: Two food trucks track their sales on the same chart. Truck A started strong but grows slowly; Truck B started at zero and climbs fast. The manager's question — “when does B catch A?” — is answered by one glance: where the two lines **cross**. Graphing a system turns algebra into a picture with the answer sitting at the intersection.
lessons.objective: Solve a system by graphing both lines and reading the intersection, then verifying the point in both equations.
concept: **Method:** graph each equation (intercept + slope-walk, from Linear Functions), find the crossing point, and read its coordinates.
System $y=2x-1$ and $y=-x+5$: the lines cross at $(2,3)$. That's the solution.
**Always verify** — graphs can deceive at grid edges: $2(2)-1=3$ ✓ and $-(2)+5=3$ ✓. Both pass, so $(2,3)$ is confirmed.
Graphing is fast and visual but only trustworthy when the crossing lands on clean grid points — for messy fractions you'll want substitution or elimination (next lessons).
lessons.keyIdea: Graph both, read the crossing, then CONFIRM by substituting into both equations.

facts.title: Its limit
facts.latex: fraction crossings are hard to read

commonMistakes.correction: A crossing that LOOKS like $(2,3)$ might be $(1.9, 3.1)$ — always substitute to confirm.
The solution is a PAIR: $x=2$ AND $y=3$, written $(2,3)$.

steps.title: Find the crossing
steps.teach: Here is $y=2x-1$ (orange) and $y=-x+5$ (blue). The marked point is the solution — move the blue line and the crossing (and the check beneath it) updates live.

steps.title: Three steps
beats: ① Graph line 1: intercept, then slope-walk.
② Graph line 2 the same way.
③ Read the crossing — that pair is the solution.
Then verify it in BOTH equations.

steps.title: The substitution check
beats: Now it's not a guess — it's confirmed.

steps.title: Graph, read, verify
steps.intro: The check is part of the method.

problems.prompt: Graphing struggles most when the solution is…
problems.explanation: Try reading $(\frac{7}{3}, \frac{11}{3})$ off a grid — algebra handles those better.

steps.title: The 17th-century mashup
steps.body: Graphing a system works because of Descartes' big idea: geometry and algebra are the SAME subject wearing different clothes. A point is a pair of numbers; a line is an equation. Two truths about numbers become two lines — and “solve” becomes “look where they cross.”

steps.title: What you learned
points: Graph both lines; the crossing is the solution.
Verify the pair in both equations — always.
Messy crossings → switch to algebraic methods.

lessons.title: Solving by Substitution
lessons.concreteComparison: One friend says “my number is 3 more than yours.” If your number is $x$, hers is literally $x+3$ — so wherever her number appears, you can **swap in** $x+3$. That swap is substitution: use one equation to rewrite the other so only one variable remains. Then it's just a linear equation, and you already own those.
lessons.objective: Solve systems by substituting one equation into the other, reducing two unknowns to one — then back-substitute for the full pair.
concept: **When one equation is solved for a variable** — like $y=x+3$ — substitute that expression into the OTHER equation: $x+(x+3)=9 \to 2x+3=9 \to x=3$.
**Back-substitute** to finish: $y=3+3=6$. Solution $(3,6)$.
If neither equation starts solved, pick the easiest variable to isolate first (a coefficient of 1 is the friendliest).
The check never changes: $(3,6)$ in both originals — $3+6=9$ ✓ and $6=3+3$ ✓.
lessons.keyIdea: Swap one equation's expression into the other → one variable → solve → back-substitute.

commonMistakes.text: Substituting back into the SAME equation.
commonMistakes.correction: Plugging $y=x+3$ into itself gives $x+3=x+3$ — true and useless. Substitute into the OTHER equation.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting parentheses around the swapped expression.

steps.title: Back-substitute
beats: Half done — a solution is a PAIR.

steps.title: Parentheses protect signs
beats: Dropping them ($2x-x+1$) would poison the sign.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.intro: Swap into the OTHER equation, then back-substitute.

steps.title: Substitution is how computers solve everything
steps.body: Solvers inside spreadsheets, game engines, and AI training loops reduce big systems the same way you just did — express one unknown in terms of others, swap it through, repeat. A million-variable system falls to the same move you used on two.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Substitute the isolated expression into the OTHER equation.
Solve the one-variable equation, then back-substitute.
Parentheses around the swap protect the signs.

lessons.title: Solving by Elimination
lessons.concreteComparison: Two receipts from the same café: $2$ coffees $+$ $1$ muffin $= \$7$; and $1$ coffee $+$ $1$ muffin $= \$5$. Subtract the receipts! The muffins cancel, leaving $1$ coffee $= \$2$. Lining up equations and adding or subtracting them to **wipe out a variable** — that's elimination, and sometimes it's dramatically faster than substitution.
lessons.objective: Solve systems by adding or subtracting equations to eliminate a variable — multiplying one equation first when needed.
concept: **Aligned opposites cancel.** $\begin{cases}x+y=7\\ x-y=3\end{cases}$: ADD them — the $y$s cancel: $2x=10 \to x=5$, then $y=2$.
**Matching terms subtract away.** $2x+y=7$ and $x+y=5$: SUBTRACT — the $y$s vanish: $x=2$, then $y=3$.
**No match yet? Multiply first.** $x+2y=8$ and $2x+3y=13$: double the first ($2x+4y=16$), subtract the second: $y=3$, then $x=2$.
Add when the target terms have OPPOSITE signs, subtract when they MATCH. Finish, as always, by checking the pair in both originals.
lessons.keyIdea: Add or subtract whole equations so one variable cancels; multiply an equation first if nothing lines up.

workedExamples.solution: Double the first: $2x+4y=16$; subtract the second: $y=3$; then $x=2$. Check: $2+6=8$ ✓, $4+9=13$ ✓.

commonMistakes.text: Subtracting only part of an equation.
commonMistakes.correction: Elimination hits EVERY term: $(2x+y)-(x+y)=7-5$ — left sides AND right sides.

commonMistakes.text: Adding when the terms match (instead of subtracting).
commonMistakes.correction: $+y$ plus $+y$ makes $2y$, not $0$ — matching terms need SUBTRACTION to cancel.

steps.title: Subtract the receipts
beats: $1$ coffee $=\$2$ — and the muffin must be $\$3$.

steps.title: Add or subtract?
steps.prompt: To eliminate $y$ from $4x+y=14$ and $2x+y=8$, you should…

steps.title: Cancel a variable
steps.intro: Line up, then add or subtract whole equations.

steps.title: Eliminate!
steps.intro: Opposites add; twins subtract; multiply to matchmake.

steps.title: Nine chapters, two thousand years
steps.body: Elimination appears fully-formed in the ancient Chinese text “Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art” (~200 BCE) — solving grain-and-livestock systems on counting boards, centuries before European algebra. The West rediscovered it and named it after Gauss; the boards had it first.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Add to cancel opposites; subtract to cancel twins.
Nothing lines up? Multiply an equation first.

lessons.title: One, None, or Infinitely Many
lessons.concreteComparison: Remember equations whose $x$ vanished — leaving $5=9$ (impossible) or $6=6$ (always true)? Systems inherit the same three endings, and now you can SEE them: lines that **cross** (one solution), **parallel** lines that never touch (no solution), and two equations that secretly draw the **same line** (infinitely many). The algebra and the picture tell one story.
lessons.objective: Classify a system as having one, none, or infinitely many solutions — from the graph (cross / parallel / same line) and from the algebra (slopes and intercepts).
concept: **One solution:** different slopes → the lines must cross exactly once. $y=2x+1$ and $y=-x+4$ cross at $(1,3)$.
**No solution:** same slope, different intercepts → parallel. $y=2x+1$ and $y=2x-3$ stay $4$ apart forever. Algebra agrees: subtracting gives $0=4$ ✗.
**Infinitely many:** the same line in disguise. $y=2x+1$ and $2y=4x+2$ — divide the second by 2 and it IS the first. Every point on the line works.
Quick test: compare slopes first; if they match, compare intercepts.
lessons.keyIdea: Different slopes → one; same slope, different b → none; same slope AND b → infinitely many.

facts.title: Parallel / same
facts.explanation: Different b → none; same b → infinite.

workedExamples.solution: Slopes differ ($2 \neq -1$) → exactly one; they cross at $(1,3)$.
Same slope, different intercepts → parallel, NO solution. (Subtracting gives $0=4$, impossible.)
Divide the second by 2: $y=2x+1$ — the SAME line. Infinitely many. (Check $x=3$: both give $7$.)

commonMistakes.text: Calling parallel lines “one solution at infinity”.
commonMistakes.correction: In this course parallel = NO solution — there is no actual point on both lines.

commonMistakes.text: Missing a same-line system because it's disguised.
commonMistakes.correction: $2y=4x+2$ doesn't LOOK like $y=2x+1$ until you simplify — always reduce before comparing.

tryIt.solution: Same slope 3, different intercepts — none. (At $x=1$: $5$ vs $2$.)
Divide by 3: same line — infinitely many. (At $x=2$: both $6$.)

steps.title: Make all three happen
steps.teach: Start: the lines cross once. Now set the blue slope to $2$ (parallel — the solution disappears), then set its intercept to $1$ (same line — every point works). Three endings, one dial away.

steps.title: Count them
steps.explanation: Same slope 5, different intercepts: parallel lines never meet.

problems.explanation: Slopes differ ($2$ vs $-2$) — they cross (at $(0,3)$, in fact).

problems.prompt: A system of two PARALLEL streets has…
problems.explanation: Parallel means never meeting — no solution.

steps.title: Full circle
steps.body: In Linear Equations you met $5=9$ (no solution) and $6=6$ (always true) — and we promised they were geometry in disguise. Here's the payoff: eliminate a variable from a parallel system and you get exactly $0=4$; from a same-line system, $0=0$. The strange equations WERE parallel and identical lines all along.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Same line (simplify to see it!) → infinitely many.

lessons.title: Systems in the Wild
lessons.concreteComparison: A school play sells $200$ tickets and collects $\$1100$. Adult tickets cost $\$8$, student tickets $\$4$. How many of each? TWO unknowns — so one equation can't do it. But the story hands you two facts: a **count** ($a+s=200$) and a **value** ($8a+4s=1100$). Two facts, two unknowns — a system. Solve it and the box office is balanced: $75$ adults, $125$ students.
lessons.objective: Translate two-fact word problems into systems (count + value, sum + difference, break-even), solve with your method of choice, and interpret the answer.
concept: **Name both unknowns** first: $a$ = adult tickets, $s$ = student tickets.

**Count + value** is the classic shape: one equation counts items ($a+s=200$), the other totals their worth ($8a+4s=1100$). Elimination: quadruple the count ($4a+4s=800$), subtract: $4a=300 \to a=75$, so $s=125$.
**Sum + difference** stories ($x+y=20$, $x-y=4$) love elimination — add and the difference melts.
**Break-even** stories (two plans, when equal?) set two expressions equal — substitution's home turf. Whatever the method: answer the actual question, with units, and sanity-check it.
lessons.keyIdea: Two unknowns need two facts: name the variables, write count/value (or sum/difference) equations, solve, interpret.

workedExamples.statement: 200 tickets, $\$1100$ collected; adults $\$8$, students $\$4$. How many of each?
workedExamples.solution: $a+s=200$, $8a+4s=1100$. Multiply the count by 4 and subtract: $4a=300 \to a=75$, $s=125$. Check: $8(75)+4(125)=1100$ ✓.

workedExamples.statement: Two numbers: sum $20$, difference $4$. Find them.
Gym A: $\$40$ joining $+ \$10$/mo. Gym B: $\$10$ joining $+ \$15$/mo. When do they cost the same?

commonMistakes.text: Writing one equation with two unknowns and getting stuck.
commonMistakes.correction: Two unknowns need TWO independent facts — reread the story for the second (a total, a difference, a comparison).

commonMistakes.text: Reporting x when the question asked for y (or both).
commonMistakes.correction: $a=75$ isn't the full answer to “how many of each” — finish the pair and label it: 75 adult, 125 student.

tryIt.statement: A jar has 25 coins — nickels and dimes — worth $\$2.00$. How many dimes?

steps.title: Why one equation can't
beats: “How many adults AND students?” — two unknowns.
Two unknowns ⇒ hunt for two facts. Always.

steps.title: Set it up
steps.prompt: 30 coins (nickels + dimes) worth $\$2.25$. Which system fits (in cents)?
steps.explanation: One equation counts the coins; the other counts the cents.

steps.title: When two plans tie
beats: Before 6: A costs more; after: B does.

steps.title: Story → system → answer
steps.intro: Name variables; find both facts; solve; interpret.

examples.prompt: A farm has chickens and cows — 20 heads, 56 legs. How many cows?

steps.title: Wild systems
steps.intro: Two unknowns, two facts, one answer.

problems.prompt: 12 pizzas — small $\$9$, large $\$14$ — cost $\$133$ total. How many large?
Sum 15, difference 3. The larger number is…

steps.title: You solve systems now
steps.body: Graphing, substitution, elimination — and the judgment of which to reach for. Count-value, sum-difference, break-even: the wild ones all fall the same way.

steps.title: You've finished “Systems of Linear Equations”
steps.body: Two equations, one plane, and every ending mapped: crossing, parallel, identical. One topic left in Grade 8: **Scatter Plots & Bivariate Data** — where messy real-world points meet the lines you now command.

practice.statement: Two numbers: sum 22, difference 8. Find them.

testYourself.statement: Adult tickets $\$10$, child $\$6$; 50 tickets, $\$420$ collected. How many adult?
Two plans: $\$18$ up front plus $\$5$/month, or no fee and $\$8$/month. When do they cost the same?
